figures.caption: TABLE I: Leading-order asymptotic approximations for the contact-line positions, and the associated timescales, in regimes I–V for spreading in a parabolic channel. The derivations of the approximations can be found in Appendix B.

FIG. 7: (a) Solutions of Eqs (41𝑎–𝑑) describing the outer-region dynamics in a Gaussian channel, with 𝐻 = 1 −M 𝑓 (𝜂) and similarity variable 𝜂 = 𝑠(M/𝑡)1/2, shown for increasing values of 𝛽 ≡ M2𝜆. The solutions were obtained using the shooting scheme outlined in Appendix C. (b) Corresponding eigenvalue 𝜂𝑢 of the similarity solution as a function of 𝛽. The dashed blue and red curves denote the asymptotic approximations in the limits 𝛽 ≪ 1 and 𝛽 ≫ 1, respectively.

FIG. 3: Illustration of five asymptotic regimes of spreading in a parabolic channel. Each row corresponds to one regime, with the timescales for each indicated by the vertical arrow. Column 1 shows schematics of the spatial regions of 𝐻 in the small-M limit with 𝜆 ≪ 1, indicating the lengthscales of the asymptotic regions and the characteristic magnitudes of 𝐻. Column 2 shows solutions of Eqs (33𝑎–𝑒) for M = 0.1 and 𝜆 = 1 in cylindrical coordinates, with black curves marking the parabolic channel boundaries.

FIG. 6: Numerical solutions of Eqs (33𝑎–𝑒) for a parabolic channel with M = 10−2 and 𝜆 = 10−1. Panels (a–e) show representative times spanning regimes I–V; coloured curves denote numerical solutions, while black curves indicate the corresponding asymptotic predictions from Appendix B. (a) Regime I, 𝑡 = {1, 2.5, 5, 7.5, 10}; analytical approximation Eq. (B5). (b) Regime II, 𝑡 = {10, 25, 50, 75, 100}; hyperbolic approximations Eqs (B9) and (B11). (c) Regime III, 𝑡 = {1, 2, 4, 8, 10} × 102; dashed curves show the predicted exponential decay Eq. (B21). (d) Regime IV, 𝑡 = {1, 1.5, 2, 2.5, 2.8} × 103; composite approximation combining Eqs (B27, B30𝑎, 𝑏, B36) (coloured curves) is almost indistinguishable from simulations (black curves). (e) Regime V, 𝑡 = {4, 5, 6, 7, 8} × 103; approximation Eq. (B42). (f) Evolution of the interface length projected onto the centreline, Δ𝑆(𝑡) = 𝑆𝑢 (𝑡) − 𝑆𝑙 (𝑡). Dashed red and blue curves show the regime II and III approximations, respectively, derived from the corresponding expressions for 𝑆𝑢 (𝑡) and 𝑆𝑙 (𝑡) (see Table I).

FIG. 1: Schematic of the two thin channel geometries considered in this paper, defined by the curvature of their centrelines (dashed curves): (a) parabolic; (b) Gaussian. In (a), the basis vectors of the cylindrical reference frame, together with the curvilinear coordinate system (𝑠∗, 𝑛∗, 𝜃) used to formulate the model, are shown (the azimuthal basis vector eθ, which points into the page, is omitted). The local inclination of the centreline to the horizontal, 𝜑, is also indicated. Injection occurs from a point source at 𝑟∗ = 0, 𝑧∗ = 0, and the light-blue region denotes the gas-filled domain. The intersections of the interface with the upper and lower channel boundaries occur at 𝑠∗ = 𝑆∗𝑢 (𝑡∗) and 𝑠∗ = 𝑆∗ 𝑙 (𝑡∗), respectively.

FIG. 10: Numerical solutions of Eqs (33𝑎–𝑒) with (40) in a Gaussian channel for M = 0.1 and 𝜆 = 104 [𝛽 = M2𝜆 = 102]. (a) The interface is plotted in cylindrical coordinates at times 𝑡 = {0, 1, 2, 4, 7}. (b) Evolution of the upper (black solid) and lower (black dash-dotted) contact lines. The early time asymptotic prediction for 𝑆𝑢 from Eq. (C3) (cyan dashed), and late-time approximation Eq. (44) (magenta dashed) are shown for comparison. (c) Interface profiles 𝐻 (𝑠, 𝑡) at times 𝑡 = {7, 102, 103, 5 × 103, 104}. (d) The profiles from (c) replotted in the self-similar variables of Eqs (C13𝑎–𝑐), 𝜉 = 𝑠/(𝜆1/4𝑡1/2) and 𝐻 = 1 − 𝜆−1/2𝑔(𝜉). The dashed red curve shows the similarity solution of Eqs (C13𝑎–𝑐), computed using a shooting method.

FIG. 5: Evolution of the contact lines in a parabolic channel, obtained by numerically solving Eqs (33𝑎–𝑒) for M = 0.1 and 𝐹0 = 0.8 at decreasing values of 𝜆. In all plots, solid lines and dashed-dotted lines correspond to the numerical solution for 𝑆𝑢 and 𝑆𝑙 respectively. Dashed lines indicate approximations from the different asymptotic regimes with each approximation labelled and matched by colour. In (a), the approximations from regime I for the contact lines (Table I) are shown in dashed red, and the asymptote for both contact lines predicted by regimes IV and V [Eq. (B34)] is shown in dashed black. Panel (b) replots the same data for 𝑆𝑢 from (a) in the coordinates of region II𝑏, 𝑡 = 𝜆𝑡 and 𝑆𝑢 = (𝜆M)1/2𝑆𝑢, with the dashed pink curve representing the exponential approximation for 𝑆𝑢 in this regime [Eq. B12]. Panel (c) replots the same data for 𝑆𝑙 from (a) in the coordinates of regions III𝑎 and IV𝑎, with 𝑡 = M𝜆𝑡 and 𝑆𝑙 = 𝜆1/2𝑆𝑙 . The dashed pink curve represents the approximation in Eq. (B19), while the black dot indicates 𝑡𝑐, the solution of Eq. (B37), marking when 𝑆𝑙 catches up with the moving front. Beyond this point, the lower contact line propagates with the front according to Eq. (B43𝑎), shown in dashed black.

TABLE II: Summary of the dynamical regimes identified for spreading in a parabolic channel, expressed in terms of dimensional parameters and variables for 𝜇𝑔 ≪ 𝜇𝑤 and 𝑘0Δ𝜌𝑔ℎ 2 ≪ 𝑞𝜇𝑔 . Column 2 lists the characteristic times that define each regime (with ∼ denoting comparable magnitude), while Columns 3 and 4 give the corresponding approximations of the lower and upper contact lines, respectively.

FIG. 9: Numerical solutions of Eqs (33𝑎–𝑒) with (40) in a Gaussian channel for M = 0.1 and 𝜆 = 102 [𝛽 = M2𝜆 = 1]. (a) The interface is shown in cylindrical coordinates at times 𝑡 = {0, 1, 4, 6, 10}, with increasing time indicated by a light-to-dark colour gradient. The channel boundaries are shown in black. (b) Evolution of the upper (black solid) and lower (black dash-dotted) contact lines. The asymptotic prediction for 𝑆𝑢 from Eq. (C3) (cyan dashed) and the late-time approximation Eq. (43) (magenta dashed) are shown for comparison. (c) Interface profiles 𝐻 (𝑠, 𝑡) at times 𝑡 = {10, 102, 103, 5 × 103, 104}. (d) The profiles from (c) replotted in the outer self-similar variables of Eqs (41𝑎–𝑑), 𝜂 = 𝑠(M/𝑡)1/2 and 𝐻 = 1 −M 𝑓 (𝜂). The dashed red curve shows the outer similarity solution of Eqs (41𝑎–𝑑), obtained via the shooting scheme outlined in Appendix C, while the dotted green curves show the inner solution Eq. (C4).

FIG. 4: Numerical solutions of spreading in a parabolic channel for M = 10−2, 𝜆 = 1 and 𝐹0 = 0.8. (a) Evolution of the interface at times 𝑡 = {1, 10, 50, 300, 500}. These profiles correspond to regimes I–V, respectively. Solid red curves show the solution of the small-slope equations (33𝑎–𝑒); dotted curves show the corresponding solution of the full composite equations (30𝑎–𝑒) for 𝜖 = 10−2 (black) and 𝜖 = 10−1 (blue). (b) Evolution of the upper and lower contact lines from the composite equations (30𝑎–𝑒) for 𝜖 = 10−2. Dashed red and dashed blue curves show the asymptotic predictions for regimes II and III, respectively (Table I).

FIG. 2: A numerical solution of (a) the small-slope model Eq. (33𝑎–𝑒) and (b) the composite model Eq. (30𝑎–𝑒) with 𝜖 = 10−2, for M = 0.1, 𝜆 = 0.1 and 𝐹0 = 0.8 in a parabolic channel [Eq. (38)]. The evolution of the upper and lower contact lines is shown by the solid black curves. The colour map represents the interface height 𝐻 (𝑠, 𝑡); for each time 𝑡, the vertical variation of colour indicates the spatial variation of 𝐻 across the interface, from 𝑆𝑙 to 𝑆𝑢. Five asymptotic regimes (I–V) are identified in (a). Times 𝜆−1 and (𝜆M)−1 are also illustrated.

TABLE III: Table of dimensional and dimensionless model parameters. Fluid properties for hydrogen were obtained from Castellucci et al. [15], and for CO2 from Bickle et al. [26]. The channel width and radial extent of a representative anticline follow Mortimer et al. [23] and Harati et al. [27]. Hydrogen injection rates reflect values typically used in numerical simulations due to limited field data [27, 28], while CO2 rates are based on estimates of injection rates at current sites of 1–2 Mt yr−1 [26]. Permeability values correspond to typical ranges of aquifers suitable for storage [15].

FIG. 8: Numerical solutions of Eqs (33𝑎–𝑒) with (40) in a Gaussian channel for M = 0.1 and 𝜆 = 10−2 [𝛽 = M2𝜆 = 10−4]. (a) The interface is shown in cylindrical coordinates at times 𝑡 = {0, 1, 10, 20, 30}, with increasing time following the light-to-dark colour gradient. The channel boundaries are shown in black. (b) Evolution of the upper (black solid) and lower (black dash-dotted) contact lines. The late-time asymptotic prediction for 𝑆𝑢 from Eq. (42𝑎) (magenta dashed) and the late-time approximation for 𝑆𝑙 Eq. (42𝑏) (green dashed) are shown for comparison. (c) Interface profiles 𝐻 (𝑠, 𝑡) at times 𝑡 = {30, 102, 103, 5 × 103, 104}. (d) The profiles from (c) replotted in the self-similar variables of Eq. (C6𝑎), 𝜂 = 𝑠(M/𝑡)1/2 and 𝐻 = 1 −M 𝑓 (𝜂). The dashed red curve shows the analytical similarity solution Eq. (C12).
